resources.string: A common intertidal mollusk with a dark blue or black shell.

A large, heavy-shelled clam common in the Pacific Northwest. The minimum harvest size for butter clams is 55 mm. If you rebury this species, bury it 25 cm deep in the same hole you found it, with the short side facing down and the long side facing up.
A large mussel found on wave-swept rocky shores.
The world\'s largest burrowing clam, known for its long siphon.
A large clam that can weigh up to 4 pounds. Also known as the Pacific Gaper.

A popular edible clam with a round, thick shell. The minimum harvest size for littleneck clams is 35 mm. If you rebury this species, bury it 5 cm deep in the same hole you found it, with the short side facing down and the long side facing up.

Also known as the Japanese littleneck, it has a distinctive cross-hatched shell pattern. The minimum harvest size for manila clams is 35 mm. If you rebury this species, bury it 5 cm deep in the same hole you found it, with the short side facing down and the long side facing up.
A rare and protected species with a beautiful iridescent shell interior. Harvesting of this species is prohibited.
A large cockle with prominent radial ribs. If you rebury this species, bury it 5 cm deep in the same hole you found it, with the short side facing down and the long side facing up.
The only oyster native to the Pacific coast of North America. Under the Species at Risk Act, the Olympia Oyster is listed as a species of Special Concern and is illegal to harvest.
The most widely farmed oyster in the world. To avoid harvesting the protected Olympia Oyster, only harvest pacific oysters larger than 5 cm in length.
A small, colorful scallop found in deeper waters.
A scallop species noted for its purple hue at the hinge of the shell interior. Also commonly known as the purple hinged rock scallop.
An oblong clam found on sandy beaches, known for its speed burrowing.
Known for its thin, fragile shell and long siphon.
A scallop with distinctive spines or ribs on its shell.
An invasive species with a thin, dark brown periostracum resembling varnish.
The largest scallop species in the world, found in the North Pacific.
